schools.summary: K&D reconstruct the disguise-plot and the appeal to Ahijah; Ellicott notes the name Abijah ('whose father is Jehovah') and its coincidence with Rehoboam's son.
Gill reads the sickness as God smiting the child in punishment of Jeroboam's sin.
Matthew Henry: the first stroke in God's controversy with Jeroboam; sickness in a family should prompt inquiry into harboured sin.
Clarke: the sickness is a prelude to greater miseries, yet also a merciful warning to turn Jeroboam from idolatry.
K&D: Jeroboam turns to Ahijah because his kingship-prophecy came true; Ellicott: blind Ahijah at Shiloh stands aloof from the 'sin of Jeroboam.'
Gill notes the wife's name is deliberately unrecorded.
Ellicott: the rustic present accords with custom but risks turning prophecy into merchandise; Clarke describes the perforated 'cracknels.'
Gill: the plain, country gifts are calculated to conceal the queen's rank.
K&D and Ellicott: Ahijah's blindness is amaurosis/cataract, making natural recognition impossible — yet God had already forewarned him.
Gill: the queen, moved by obedience and a mother's love, reaches a prophet who cannot see her.
K&D: the Lord tells Ahijah in advance both her coming and what to say, arming the blind man against her pretence.
Gill: the revelation came by dream or inward impulse before she arrived.
Clarke: the disguise is a confession — it would discredit Jeroboam's calves to be known to consult a prophet of Jehovah.

themes: secret return to the true God

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: the prophet greets her by name, with no regard to rank, present, or concealment; God judges men by what they are, not what they seem.
K&D: recognition hangs on the sound of her feet at the door, not on sight.
Gill: it was the noise of her feet entering that prompted the greeting.
K&D lay out the oracle's structure; 'cast me behind thy back' (only here and Ezekiel 23:35) marks utter contempt of God.
Matthew Henry: the charge is one of ingratitude — God recounts the mercy of the kingdom to convict the ungrateful; the wife alone could carry such a message.
Gill identifies the addressee and lets the sentence run into the doom.

themes: the conditional gift of the kingdom

schools.summary: K&D: David is the measure — he kept God's commandments and followed with all his heart, unlike Jeroboam.
Clarke: God 'rent' the kingdom means he permitted it to be rent for Rehoboam's folly; Jeroboam was instrument, not author.
Gill quantifies the gift — ten parts out of twelve — to sharpen the disproportion.
Gill: Jeroboam's sin surpasses Saul (no idolatry) and Solomon (who did not draw the people in); he first made idolatry the state religion.
Ellicott: the strong language is justified by the contempt of God's conditional promise and, chiefly, by the unprecedented, lasting effect — idolatry that 'made Israel to sin.'

debates.question: How can Jeroboam's sin be 'above all that were before thee'?

sides.position: by comparison man-by-man — worse than Saul and Solomon, being the first to institutionalise idolatry
by its unprecedented and enduring effect, poisoning the nation's worship for centuries

schools.summary: Ellicott and K&D parse the idioms for 'every male' and the contemptuous 'as dung' — the house of Jeroboam swept away like refuse.
Matthew Henry: the worshipper of dunghill-deities has his family removed as a dunghill; wicked royal houses are no better in God's account.
Clarke: the blunt phrase means simply 'every male'; none is spared.
Gill: God will bring calamity, destruction, and ruin on the house.
Ellicott: dogs are city scavengers, fowls are vultures; the horror of unburied dead deepened by Israel's honouring of the body as consecrated to God; K&D note the recurring formula of judgment.
Clarke: to be eaten by dogs and birds is to be denied honourable burial in the fathers' sepulchres.
Gill: the meaning is simply that they should have no burial.
Matthew Henry: the child's death at her homecoming is a sign confirming the rest of the threatenings, as with Eli's sons.
Gill: she is bidden home with all haste, hurried back to meet the fate she came to escape.
K&D confirm the plain tie between the boy's death and the instant of her return.
Matthew Henry: a little grace goes a great way; the righteous are removed from the evil to come; God takes soonest those he loves best.
Ellicott: early death in peace is the only reward for piety in coming judgment (like Josiah); not the Greek view that early death is Heaven's ordinary blessing.
Clarke: God will not destroy the righteous with the wicked and respects even a little good, as a seed from himself.
Gill: all Israel mourn the hopeful heir on whom hopes of a better reign had rested.
Ellicott identifies Baasha and canvasses the disputed 'but what? even now'; K&D read it as Ahijah interrupting himself — the avenger is already at hand.
Gill: 'Baasha is meant,' pointing forward to the fulfilment.

debates.question: What does the phrase 'but what? even now' mean?

sides.position: 'even now' — the judgment is already at hand (compare Luke 12:49)
'in this day and in this time' / 'what is now and what shall be' (Vulgate, Targum)

schools.summary: K&D expand the reed-in-water simile and the exile 'beyond the river'; Ellicott names it the first prophecy of captivity and corrects 'groves' to Asherah-idols.
Gill reads the reed of the fluctuating, war-torn condition of Israel under future dynasties.
Clarke points to the partial fulfilment on Jeroboam's own house.
K&D: 'give Israel up' for Jeroboam's sins, carried at once into the fulfilment of the child's death and the reign's close.
Matthew Henry: families and kingdoms are ruined by the sin of their heads; leaders answer for the sins they draw others into.
Gill: God gives Israel up into the hands of their enemies for the double sin of Jeroboam.

themes: corporate consequence of a leader's sin

schools.summary: Ellicott locates Tirzah, the beautiful capital (Song 6:4) later replaced by Samaria, at modern Telluzah.
Matthew Henry: death cannot be shut out even from so delightful a place.
Gill: the queen arose and departed under the prophet's heavy word.
K&D stress the governing clause — all Israel mourned and buried him 'according to the word of the Lord' by Ahijah; the emphasis is on fulfilment.
Gill: the one member of the house given an honourable burial suited to his rank.

themes: the word of the Lord fulfilled

schools.summary: Ellicott: the historian records only exaltation, idolatry, and punishment; a reign perhaps prosperous is summed up as 'who made Israel to sin.'
Gill fills 'how he warred' with the wars against Rehoboam and Abijam.
Clarke refers the reader to Chronicles for the omitted particulars.
K&D record the twenty-two years and the succession of Nadab, through whom Ahijah's sentence begins to work.
Gill: 'slept with his fathers' covers a death under judgment — the Chronicler says the Lord struck him — and the house falls within two years.
Matthew Henry: privilege wasted — formed in Solomon's best days yet corrupted by his defection; the twice-named Ammonitess warns against the unequal yoke.
Ellicott notes the coincidence of the name Abijah in the sons of Jeroboam and Rehoboam.
Gill marks the bare fact that Rehoboam reigned over Judah's two tribes.
K&D and Ellicott: a three-year reformation gave way to reckless apostasy, ascribed to the people under a weak king — the harvest of Solomon's seed.
Matthew Henry: Judah, the only professing people, provoked God to jealousy like an adulterous wife, sinning above their fathers.
Gill fixes the turn at the end of the third year of Rehoboam's reign.

themes: a weak king, a guilty people

schools.summary: K&D and Ellicott trace the mazzeboth (memorial pillars) and asherahs from lawful emblem to forbidden idol, the grosser idolatry meant here.
Gill: Judah, with the temple in its own tribe, had no excuse for the high places.
K&D, Ellicott, and Clarke expound qadesh ('consecrated') — cultic prostitution sanctioned by ritual, the climax of Judah's corruption and the sin of the dispossessed Canaanites.
Gill: such filthy practices usually attended heathen idolatry in temples and groves.
K&D and Ellicott: Shishak (Sheshonk) invades and spares Jerusalem after Shemaiah's call to repentance; the campaign is confirmed by the Karnak inscription and perhaps instigated by Jeroboam.
Gill fixes the invasion two years after the apostasy, as chastisement.
K&D and Ellicott: the utter spoil of Solomon's golden treasures, by surrender not sack; Ellicott refutes the theory that the invasion destroyed Judah's archives.
Clarke: the three hundred golden shields were an immense booty of beaten gold.
Gill suspects Jeroboam, Shishak's old friend, informed and drew him against Rehoboam.
Ellicott: the brazen shields symbolise a Judah whose 'fine gold had become brass' — the semblance of glory over real impoverishment.
Gill: the plain reason is that Rehoboam, stripped of gold, could not replace the golden shields.
K&D: the king went to the temple with solemn pomp; Ellicott and Clarke: Rehoboam still worshipped there and, after the invasion, humbled himself and returned to the Lord.
Gill: he had not wholly forsaken the temple, and the humbling providence may have stirred him to attend more.
K&D and Ellicott point to the fuller sources — the Chronicles, the books of Shemaiah and Iddo — and gloss the 'war' as settled hostility.
Gill: the formula gathers up all that was memorable in Rehoboam's reign.
K&D and Ellicott, given Shemaiah's ban on invasion and the silence of the record, read 'war' as continued enmity rather than pitched battle.
Gill: no offensive war, but a defensive one, with border skirmishes and bickerings.

sides.position: settled hostility and broken relations, not actual battle
real if indecisive fighting — defensive war and border skirmishes

schools.summary: Gill: the Ammonitess mother is named again as the leading step to Rehoboam's idolatry and the means of his continuing in it.
Clarke presses heredity — an impure fountain, a wicked son — yet guards it: grace may be grafted on a crab stock; do no evil that good may come.
K&D: the repeated name may reflect the writer's sources; 'Abijam' is the older form of 'Abijah.'
